text.title: db-weaviate Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for db-weaviate as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Mixing weaviate-client v3 and v4 code in the same project because "the old integration still works".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: v3 and v4 APIs are incompatible; integrations written for v3 (common in older LangChain/LlamaIndex versions) will fail silently or crash when called against v4 client objects.

failure-mode.description: Using Weaviate's auto-vectorizer modules (text2vec-openai, text2vec-cohere) because "it saves one step" in the pipeline.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Auto-vectorizer modules add latency at index time, couple the schema to external API keys, and make it impossible to experiment with different embedding models without dropping and re-creating the collection. Schema changes require collection drop in Weaviate v4 — a costly operation for large indices.

failure-mode.description: Skipping batch.dynamic() in production because "we only insert once a day".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Insert throughput is still measured in documents per second; serial inserts block the application during the entire ingestion window. Using batch.dynamic() costs nothing and provides a 10x speedup as a free benefit of the API design.

failure-mode.description: Using alpha=0.5 for all queries without classifying the query type (keyword vs. semantic).
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Query intent varies: "quick reference lookups" (user typed exact keywords) need alpha near 0 (BM25-heavy); "conceptual exploration" queries need alpha near 1 (semantic-heavy). Static alpha discards this signal and produces mediocre results for both cases.

failure-mode.description: Using raw GraphQL for simple vector searches because "it gives me more control".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Raw GraphQL requires string concatenation, is harder to test, loses type safety, and has no error handling. The Python client's near_vector() method produces the same result with better UX and easier debugging.

failure-mode.description: Storing cross-references as denormalized text fields ("author_name: John Smith") to avoid schema setup overhead.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Denormalization creates duplicates, breaks updates (if Author.email changes, all Document copies are stale), and prevents graph traversal queries. The schema overhead is one-time; the maintenance cost of denormalization is unbounded.

failure-mode.description: Assuming Weaviate v4 is backward-compatible with v3 client code because "the API looks similar".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: v4 is a rewrite; object initialization, method signatures, and error types are incompatible. Code written for v3 will import wrong modules or crash at runtime with cryptic errors (e.g., "AttributeError: module 'weaviate' has no attribute 'connect_to_local'").

failure-mode.description: Storing frequently-updated data (user session state, time-series metrics) in Weaviate because "we already have it up".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Weaviate is optimized for static/append-only vector indices, not high-churn transactional data. Update operations require recomputation of vector indices; frequent updates degrade query performance and cause BM25 rebuild storms.

failure-mode.description: Setting ef=10 (low) for "faster queries" without measuring latency trade-offs.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: ef controls the search depth in HNSW; low values (ef < 50) reduce recall to below 80% at scale. The latency gain is minimal (a few milliseconds) compared to the recall loss (often 10-20% fewer relevant results). Tune based on measured SLAs, not guesses.

failure-mode.description: Reusing the same client across async tasks without synchronization because "Weaviate's thread-safe".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Weaviate's Python client is thread-safe for concurrent queries but NOT for concurrent schema changes or batch inserts to the same collection. Concurrent writes require external locking or connection pooling.
